text: The fundamental principles of computational theory suggest that any Turing-complete system can simulate any other computational process. This profound insight underpins modern software engineering paradigms where abstraction layers allow developers to build complex systems without constant regard for underlying hardware implementations. When considering the evolution of programming languages from low-level assembly to high-level domain-specific languages, we observe a continuous trajectory toward increased expressiveness and developer productivity. text: The Church-Turing thesis posits that any effectively computable function can be computed by a Turing machine, forming the theoretical bedrock of modern computer science. This profound insight implies that all computational systems of equivalent power—whether lambda calculus, recursive functions, or quantum computing models—exhibit fundamental universality in their capacity to simulate algorithmic processes. The implications extend beyond theoretical constructs into practical software engineering; compilers for languages like C++ inherently rely on this principle by transforming high-level abstractions into machine-executable instructions through intermediate representations that preserve computational intention
title: Mrs.

text: Gradual typing integrates dynamic and static checking via consistency relations, allowing incremental hardening of scripts in languages like TypeScript or Python.
